subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Coffee in Cup Table C(t) — Rate Approximation, MVT, Midpoint Sum Avg Value & Model Rate
stimulus:
Hot water is dripping through a coffeemaker, filling a large cup with coffee. The amount of coffee in the cup at time $t$, $0 \le t \le 6$, is given by a differentiable function $C$, where $t$ is measured in minutes. Selected values of $C(t)$, measured in ounces, are given in the table.

| $t$ (minutes) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C(t)$ (ounces) | 0 | 5.3 | 8.8 | 11.2 | 12.8 | 13.8 | 14.5 |

parts.prompt: Use the data in the table to approximate $C'(3.5)$. Show the computations that lead to your answer, and indicate units of measure.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $C'(3.5) \approx \frac{C(4)-C(3)}{1} = 1.6$ ounces per minute.

parts.prompt: Is there a time $t$, $2 \le t \le 4$, at which $C'(t) = 2$? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $\frac{C(4)-C(2)}{2} = 2$. By MVT ($C$ differentiable), $\exists t \in (2,4)$ with $C'(t) = 2$. Yes.

scoring_points.description: Applies MVT: since $C$ is differentiable (continuous) on $[2, 4]$, by MVT there exists $t \in (2, 4)$ with $C'(t) = 2$

required_elements: Applies MVT: since $C$ is differentiable (continuous) on $[2, 4]$, by MVT there exists $t \in (2, 4)$ with $C'(t) = 2$

parts.prompt: Use a midpoint sum with three subintervals of equal length indicated by the data in the table to approximate the value of $\frac{1}{6}\int_0^6 C(t)\, dt$. Using correct units, explain the meaning of $\frac{1}{6}\int_0^6 C(t)\, dt$ in the context of the problem.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Midpoint sum $= \frac{1}{6}[2(5.3) + 2(11.2) + 2(13.8)] = 10.1$ ounces. Interpretation: average amount of coffee in the cup over $[0, 6]$ minutes.

alternatives.correct_example: $\frac{1}{6}\int_0^6 C(t)\, dt$ is the average amount of coffee in the cup, in ounces, over the time interval $0 \le t \le 6$ minutes

parts.prompt: The amount of coffee in the cup, in ounces, is modeled by $B(t) = 16 - 16e^{-0.4t}$. Using this model, find the rate at which the amount of coffee in the cup is changing when $t = 5$.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $B'(t) = 6.4e^{-0.4t}$; $B'(5) = \frac{6.4}{e^2}$ ounces per minute.
